column3: Holy Spirit descends on Apostles, rapid spread of Christianity begins.
column7: Seen as the "birthday" of the Church.

column3: Epistles by Paul, Peter, James, John, Jude; Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke written.
column6: Paul, Peter, John, James, Luke, Mark, Matthew
column7: Earliest Christian texts, circulated among churches.

column3: Addressed Gentile inclusion and Mosaic Law; James presides.
column7: First Christian council; set precedent for synodal decision-making.

column3: Martyrdom of Peter and Paul under Nero.
column7: Early Christian martyrdoms; persecution shapes theology of suffering.

column3: Cites at least 8 NT books, showing early canon awareness.
column7: Letter to Corinth; evidence of Roman primacy.

column3: Polycarp acknowledges 15 NT books; Ignatius acknowledges about 7.
column7: Early martyrdom accounts; strong emphasis on episcopal authority.

column3: Lists 22 of 27 NT books; early evidence of canon formation.
column7: Oldest known list of NT books.

column3: Recognizes 22 NT books.
column7: Early theologian, opponent of some heresies.

column3: Origen articulates early Trinitarian doctrine and church authority.
column7: Influential in development of biblical exegesis and theology.

column3: Refutes Gnosticism, emphasizes apostolic succession, scripture, and rule of faith.
column7: Defends fourfold Gospel, apostolic tradition.

column3: Early Catholic structure emerges under Roman direction.
column7: Advocates unity under Rome.

column3: Constantine legalizes Christianity, ending major persecutions.
column7: Christianity gains legal status in Roman Empire.

column3: Defines Christ's divinity (homoousios), rejects Arianism, issues first Nicene Creed.
column7: First ecumenical council; sets precedent for future councils.

column3: Affirms 26 NT books as canonical (excludes Revelation).
column7: Early canon list; regional influence.

column3: Practice and use of images begins.
column7: Development of Christian art and iconography.

column3: Reaffirms Nicaea, expands on Holy Spirit, defines five patriarchates.
column7: Nicene Creed expanded; defines Trinity more fully.

column3: Affirms 27 NT books as canonical.
column7: Augustine influential; first full NT canon.

column3: Confirms 27-book NT canon; includes some Apocrypha in OT.
column7: NT canon set for Western Church.

column3: Declares Mary as "Mother of God" (Theotokos); addresses Nestorianism.
column7: Third ecumenical council; Christological definitions.

column3: Defines two natures of Christ (divine and human) in one person.
column7: Fourth ecumenical council; Chalcedonian Creed.

column3: Centralizes papal authority, strengthens church's political power.
column7: Gregorian reforms; missionary activity.

column3: Established by Gregory I.
column7: Not formally defined until later, but taught by Gregory.

column3: Formal split between Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches.
column6: Pope Leo IX, Patriarch Michael Cerularius
column7: Mutual excommunications; cultural and doctrinal differences.

column3: Ratifies transubstantiation; confession to priest standardized.
column7: Major medieval reform council.

column3: Purgatory proclaimed as dogma.
column7: Attempted reunion with Eastern Church.

column3: Declares tradition and scripture co-authoritative; confirms 27 NT books, 11 Apocryphal OT books.
column7: Catholic response to Reformation; canon fixed for Catholics.

column3: Papal infallibility defined as dogma.
column7: Centralizes authority in papacy.

column3: Declared dogma by Pope Pius XII.
column7: Most recent Marian dogma.

column3: Modernizes church practices and doctrine.
